title: Space-Time Tradeoffs of Pauli-Based Computation in Distributed qLDPC Architectures
authors: Naphan Benchasattabuse, Michal Hajdušek, Rodney Van Meter
abstract: Pauli-based computation (PBC) provides a universal framework for executing fault-tolerant quantum algorithms using Pauli measurements and magic states. In monolithic architectures, the serialized nature of PBC directly ties runtime to a circuit's T-gate count, making it slow on metrics like circuit depth. However, in distributed quantum computing (DQC), the primary bottleneck is remote Bell pair generation. We investigate the tradeoff between error-correcting code block size and execution time of PBC within the Q-Fly architecture at intermediate scale, limiting individual node capacities to reflect near-term constraints while supplying abundant network nodes to minimize routing and compilation effects. We find that large qLDPC code blocks outperform the surface code baseline in terms of execution time by up to an order of magnitude when evaluated against quantum optimization algorithms. By moving groups of qubits to free nodes to bypass the sequential bottleneck of PBC, the large-block architecture minimizes network operations and achieves faster overall execution. This demonstrates that PBC is a competitive model in the distributed regime, establishing it as a practical compilation baseline for qLDPC systems before invoking more efficient transversal or homological gates.
summary: This paper investigates how to leverage the high-rate capacity of qLDPC codes in a distributed architecture to overcome the sequential bottlenecks of Pauli-based computation. It demonstrates that by using architecture-aware circuit design, one can achieve significant speedups in quantum optimization algorithms compared to traditional surface code approaches.
main_problem: Optimizing the space-time tradeoffs in distributed quantum computing (DQC) by addressing the bottleneck of slow inter-node entanglement generation in Pauli-based computation (PBC) architectures.
main_result: Large qLDPC code blocks in a Q-Fly architecture outperform the surface code baseline in execution time by up to an order of magnitude for quantum optimization algorithms like QAOA and DQI.
method: The authors use architecture-aware circuit design and analytical resource estimation, comparing custom-designed distributed circuits against a monolithic surface code baseline using the Q-Fly network topology.
model_or_system: A hierarchical Q-Fly architecture consisting of 64 groups of 12 computational nodes connected via a chordal ring graph, utilizing high-rate qLDPC (bivariate bicycle) codes and photonic interconnects for Bell pair generation.
key_observables: Logical cycle counts, execution time (latency), and network operation counts.
important_parameters: Network latency (T_Bell ranging from 2 to 10 cycles), routing diameter (max 3 hops), node capacity (~1,000 physical qubits), and rotation precision (64-bit).
main_assumptions: Assumes photonic interconnects mediate the network, nodes have sufficient local magic state factories, and routing is limited to two switch hops to maintain fidelity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the Q-Fly architecture hierarchy; Figure 2 shows the optimized synthesis of controlled rotations using the TACU gadget; Figure 3 illustrates the WDB-based Dicke state construction.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of PBC bottlenecks in DQC, defines the Q-Fly architecture and qLDPC codes, details the construction of architecture-aware subroutines (adders, rotations, fan-out), performs resource estimation for QAOA and DQI algorithms, and concludes with a comparative performance analysis against surface code architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Example of a Q-Fly architecture with ﬁve groups, each composed of four computational quantum nodes, an optical switch and a pool of BSAs.
Fig. 3: Partial circuit illustrating the controlled amplitude distribution ladder for the n-th qubit.

Fig. 2: Optimized synthesis of controlled continuous rota- tions. (a) The standard sequential fractional rotation. (b) The temporary-AND compute-uncompute (TACU) gadget, which intercepts multiple controls to bypass exponential angle frac- turing. Measurement of the ancilla in the X-basis triggers a classical feed-forward CZ correction on the controls.
